content:
# Leviticus Chapter 22 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71r–71v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 108–109
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_022.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 22 is the practical companion to the priestly-holiness legislation of chapter 21. Where chapter 21 governed the priest's body — who may serve at the altar, in what physical condition, and under what domestic arrangements — chapter 22 governs the priestly portion and the sacrificial animal: who may eat the holy food, under what conditions, and what animals may be presented. The chapter falls into three major literary blocks introduced by the divine speech formula "the LORD spoke to Moses" (vv.1, 17, 26): (1) laws governing priestly access to the holy things — *qodshei benei-Yisra'el* — with emphasis on conditions of ritual purity and the exclusion of the *zar* ("outsider"), vv.1–16; (2) laws governing the acceptability of sacrificial animals — the requirement of *tamim* ("without blemish"), the minimum age of eight days, the prohibition of slaughtering a mother-animal and its offspring on the same day, and the thank-offering's same-day consumption requirement — vv.17–30; and (3) the closing theological summary: "you shall not profane my holy name" (*ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi*) and the self-identification formula "I am the LORD who sanctifies you" — vv.31–33.

The chapter's governing logic is the holiness map traced throughout chapters 17–21: gradated access to sacred things corresponds to gradated proximity to divine presence. The same *tamim* ("without blemish/complete") demanded of the priestly body in chapter 21 is here demanded of the sacrificial animal; the same concern for profanation (*challal*) of the LORD's name runs throughout. The chapter concludes as the Holiness Code regularly does — with the self-identification "I am the LORD" — but here amplified: "I am the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God; I am the LORD" (v.33). The Exodus-redemption grounds the entire sacrificial-holiness system.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | *bney yisrael* = "sons of Israel" | *zar* = "outsider" | *tamim* = "without blemish" | *terumah* = "contribution" | *challal* = "profane" | *karet* = "cut off" | *neder* = "vow" | *nedavah* = "freewill offering" | *todah* = "thank offering" | English name forms throughout (Moses, Aaron)

---

## Consensus Translation

**22:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**22:2** "Speak to Aaron and his sons, that they separate themselves from the holy things of the sons of Israel, which they consecrate to me, and that they not profane my holy name; I am the LORD.

**22:3** Say to them: Throughout your generations, any man of all your offspring who approaches the holy things that the sons of Israel consecrate to the LORD while his uncleanness is upon him — that person shall be cut off from before me; I am the LORD.

**22:4** No man of the offspring of Aaron who is a leper or who has a discharge shall eat of the holy things until he is clean. Whoever touches anything made unclean by a corpse, or a man from whom there is an emission of semen,

**22:5** or a man who touches any swarming thing by which he becomes unclean, or a person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness —

**22:6** the person who touches any such thing shall be unclean until evening and shall not eat of the holy things unless he has bathed his body in water.

**22:7** When the sun goes down he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat of the holy things, for they are his food.

**22:8** He shall not eat what dies of itself or what is torn by beasts, making himself unclean by it; I am the LORD.

**22:9** They shall keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it and die thereby when they profane it; I am the LORD who sanctifies them.

**22:10** No outsider shall eat of the holy things; no sojourner of the priest's household nor hired servant shall eat of the holy things.

**22:11** But if a priest buys a person with his money, that person may eat of it; and those born in his house may eat of his food.

**22:12** If a priest's daughter marries an outsider, she shall not eat of the contribution of the holy things.

**22:13** But if a priest's daughter is widowed or divorced and has no offspring and returns to her father's house as in her youth, she may eat of her father's food; but no outsider shall eat of it.

**22:14** And if a man eats a holy thing inadvertently, he shall add a fifth of its value to it and give the holy thing to the priest.

**22:15** They shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Israel, which they contribute to the LORD,

**22:16** and so cause them to bear iniquity and guilt by eating their holy things; for I am the LORD who sanctifies them."

**22:17** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**22:18** "Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the sons of Israel and say to them: When any man of the house of Israel or of the sojourners in Israel presents his offering, whether in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering, which they present to the LORD as a burnt offering —

**22:19** for your acceptance, it shall be a male without blemish from the cattle, from the sheep, or from the goats.

**22:20** You shall not offer anything that has a blemish, for it will not be accepted for you.

**22:21** And when a man presents a sacrifice of peace offerings to the LORD to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering, from the herd or from the flock, it must be without blemish to be accepted; there shall be no blemish in it.

**22:22** Anything blind or broken or maimed, or having a running sore or a rash or scabs — you shall not offer these to the LORD, and you shall not give any of them as a food offering on the altar to the LORD.

**22:23** A bull or a lamb that has a limb too long or too short you may offer as a freewill offering, but for a vow it shall not be accepted.

**22:24** Any animal that has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut you shall not offer to the LORD; you shall not do this within your land.

**22:25** Neither shall you offer as the bread of your God any such animals obtained from a foreigner, for their corruption is in them — there is a blemish in them; they will not be accepted for you.

**22:26** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**22:27** "When a bull or a sheep or a goat is born, it shall remain seven days with its mother, and from the eighth day on it shall be acceptable as a food offering to the LORD.

**22:28** But you shall not slaughter an ox or a sheep and its young both on the same day.

**22:29** And when you sacrifice a thank offering to the LORD, you shall offer it so that it may be accepted on your behalf.

**22:30** It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until morning; I am the LORD.

**22:31** You shall keep my commandments and do them; I am the LORD.

**22:32** And you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified in the midst of the sons of Israel; I am the LORD who sanctifies you,

**22:33** who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God; I am the LORD."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe." LOGOS: "spoke to Moses." PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — English name form per project convention, consistent throughout the series. EMET's "Moshe" is appropriate for hyper-literal analysis but is not the project standard for consensus text.

**V. 2 — "Separate themselves" vs. "handle with care."** EMET: "they shall separate themselves [ve-yinnazeru]." LOGOS: "that they separate themselves." PNEUMA: "to handle the sacred offerings with care." Consensus: "that they separate themselves" — EMET/LOGOS. The verb *yinnazeru* is the Nifal of *n-z-r* — the same root as *nazir* (Nazirite), connoting active, deliberate separation from holy things when ritually unqualified. PNEUMA's "handle with care" flattens this root's resonance into a general practical caution, losing the precision of the consecrated-withdrawal posture. LOGOS and EMET correctly render the Nifal as "separate themselves."

**V. 2 — Conjunction placement: "which they consecrate to me, and that they not profane."** EMET: "which they consecrate to me, and they shall not profane." LOGOS: "in the things that they consecrate to me" then "not profane." PNEUMA compresses the verse into a single clause. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in preserving the verse's two-part structure: (a) separation from holy things; (b) not profaning the holy name. The verse is the chapter's topic statement and its dual scope must be preserved.

**V. 2 — "Aaron" transliteration.** EMET: "Aharon." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "Aaron." Consensus: "Aaron" — English name form per project convention.

**V. 3 — "Throughout your generations" — position.** EMET: "any man of all your offspring throughout their generations." LOGOS: "Throughout your generations, if any man of all your offspring." PNEUMA: "For all your future generations, if any of your descendants." Consensus: "Throughout your generations, any man of all your offspring" — LOGOS structure preferred. PNEUMA's "future generations" adds a qualifier not in the MT; the MT's *le-dorotam* ("throughout their generations") refers to all generations without the forward-only restriction "future" implies.

**V. 3 — "Cut off from before me" vs. "cut off from my presence."** EMET: "shall be cut off from before my face [mi-lefanai]." LOGOS: "shall be cut off from before me." PNEUMA: "must be cut off from my presence." Consensus: "shall be cut off from before me" — LOGOS. The MT has *mi-lefanai* ("from before my face"), a relational formula emphasizing exclusion from the divine presence. LOGOS's "from before me" preserves the literal spatial sense. PNEUMA's "from my presence" is a legitimate paraphrase but loses the "before" (*lefanai*) directionality. EMET's "from before my face" is accurate but slightly literal; "from before me" is preferred for the consensus.

**V. 4 — "Leper" vs. "skin-diseased person."** EMET: "skin-diseased person [tsaru'a]." LOGOS: "leper." PNEUMA: "who has a skin disease." Consensus: "leper" — LOGOS. The term *tsaru'a* technically encompasses various skin conditions (see EMET's note on tsara'at), but "leper" is the established formal translation tradition (ESV, NASB, KJV) and is consistent with the project's Leviticus 13–14 rendering. PNEUMA's "who has a skin disease" is more clinically precise but loses the legal register; "leper" is retained as the conventional rendering throughout Leviticus.

**V. 4 — "Unclean by a corpse" vs. "unclean from a corpse" vs. "made unclean by a corpse."** EMET: "anything unclean by a dead body [tame-nefesh]." LOGOS: "anything made unclean from a corpse." PNEUMA: "something made unclean by a corpse." Consensus: "anything made unclean by a corpse" — LOGOS/PNEUMA adjusted. The MT's *tame-nefesh* is a technical compound: "unclean of corpse/soul," the most potent ritual impurity. "Made unclean by a corpse" accurately renders the passive sense. EMET's "unclean by a dead body" is also accurate; LOGOS uses "from a corpse" which is equally defensible. The consensus adopts "made unclean by a corpse" as most natural.

**V. 4 — "Emission of semen" vs. "discharge of seed."** EMET: "a discharge of seed [shich'vat-zara]." LOGOS: "an emission of semen." PNEUMA: "a seminal emission." Consensus: "an emission of semen" — LOGOS. The MT's *shich'vat-zara* is "a lying of seed" — a seminal emission. LOGOS's "emission of semen" is the formal standard rendering (ESV). PNEUMA's "seminal emission" is equally precise. EMET's "discharge of seed" is literal but less conventional medically. "Emission of semen" is preferred.

**V. 5 — "Swarming thing" — agreement.** All three agents preserve the concept; EMET: "swarming thing." LOGOS: "swarming thing." PNEUMA: "swarming creature." Consensus: "swarming thing" — EMET/LOGOS. *Sherets* throughout Leviticus is consistently "swarming thing" in the project convention (cf. Lev 11).

**V. 5 — "A person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness."** EMET: "in a man who makes him unclean by any uncleanness of his." LOGOS: "any person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness." PNEUMA: "touches a person whose uncleanness — whatever it may be — makes him unclean." Consensus: "a person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness" — LOGOS. LOGOS preserves the open-ended scope of the clause (*le-khol tumato* = "by any of his uncleanness") and the general referent "person" (*adam*). EMET's "in a man" is literal for *be-adam* but slightly awkward; LOGOS's "a person" is cleaner while being equivalent. PNEUMA restructures the clause unnecessarily.

**V. 6 — "Bathe his body" vs. "bathe his flesh."** EMET: "bathed his flesh in water [rachats besaro ba-mayim]." LOGOS: "bathed his body in water." PNEUMA: "bathed himself in water." Consensus: "bathed his body in water" — LOGOS. The MT has *besaro* ("his flesh/body"). LOGOS's "his body" is the natural English equivalent. EMET's "his flesh" is literal and defensible; PNEUMA's "himself" is a free paraphrase that drops the bodily specificity present in the MT. "His body" is preferred.

**V. 7 — "For they are his food" vs. "for it is his food."** EMET: "for it is his food [ki lachmo hu]." LOGOS: "for they are his food." PNEUMA: omits this phrase entirely. Consensus: "for they are his food" — LOGOS. The MT has *ki lachmo hu* — "for his bread/food it is" — a singular pronoun, but referring collectively to the holy things (plural subject). Both "it is his food" and "they are his food" are defensible; LOGOS's plural "they are" is more natural given the plural antecedent (holy things). PNEUMA's omission of this explanatory clause is inadmissible for a consensus rendering of a verse where the rationale clause is present in the MT.

**V. 7 — "When the sun goes down" vs. "when the sun sets."** EMET: "when the sun sets [u-va ha-shemesh]." LOGOS: "When the sun goes down." PNEUMA: "When the sun has set." Consensus: "When the sun goes down" — LOGOS. The MT's *u-va ha-shemesh* is "when the sun comes/goes" — referring to its setting. LOGOS's "goes down" is the natural English rendering. EMET's "sets" is equally standard and briefer. PNEUMA's past tense "has set" implies completed action whereas the Hebrew conditional clause describes the temporal boundary. "Goes down" is preferred.

**V. 8 — "What dies of itself" vs. "a carcass" / "nevelah."** EMET: "A carcass [nevelah]." LOGOS: "what dies of itself." PNEUMA: "anything found dead." Consensus: "what dies of itself" — LOGOS. The MT's *nevelah* is an animal that died without proper slaughter; LOGOS's "what dies of itself" is the established formal rendering (ESV, NASB). EMET's "carcass" is correct but less specific — a carcass could also include properly slaughtered animals after death. "What dies of itself" is more precise.

**V. 8 — "What is torn by beasts" vs. "one torn" / "terefah."** EMET: "one torn [terefah]." LOGOS: "what is torn by beasts." PNEUMA: "torn by wild animals." Consensus: "what is torn by beasts" — LOGOS. The MT's *terefah* (from *taraf*, "to tear") literally means something torn; LOGOS's "torn by beasts" preserves the active cause. EMET's "one torn" is literal but lacks the agent. PNEUMA's "torn by wild animals" is equivalent to LOGOS. "Torn by beasts" is slightly more formal and preferred.

**V. 8 — "Making himself unclean by it" — construction.** EMET: "to make himself unclean by it." LOGOS: "becoming unclean by it." PNEUMA omits this clause. Consensus: "making himself unclean by it" — adjusted from LOGOS. The MT has *la-tame-vah bo* ("to become unclean by it"), a purposive infinitive. EMET's "to make himself unclean" and LOGOS's "becoming unclean by it" both capture this. The consensus uses "making himself unclean by it" as the participial construction that flows smoothly from the main clause.

**V. 9 — "They shall keep my charge" — opening.** EMET: "And they shall keep my charge [ve-shamru et-mishmarti]." LOGOS: "They shall therefore keep my charge." PNEUMA: "The priests must keep my charge." Consensus: "They shall keep my charge" — LOGOS structure without "therefore." The MT has *ve-shamru et-mishmarti* — "and they shall keep my charge." LOGOS adds "therefore" as an interpretive connector; while it reads naturally it is not in the MT. PNEUMA's explicit "The priests" narrows what is already understood from context but represents an addition. The subject remains implicit from the section context (the priests of vv.4–8). "They shall keep my charge" preserves the MT's direct formulation.

**V. 9 — "Lest they bear sin" vs. "and not bear sin."** EMET: "and they shall not bear sin for it [ve-lo yis'u alav chet]." LOGOS: "lest they bear sin for it." PNEUMA: "or they will bear the sin." Consensus: "lest they bear sin for it" — LOGOS. The MT's *ve-lo yis'u* is a negative purpose clause following the obligation of v.9a: "keep my charge so that they will not bear sin." LOGOS's "lest they bear sin" captures the purpose/consequence construction well. EMET's "and they shall not bear sin" is literal but reads as an additional commandment rather than a consequence. PNEUMA's "or they will bear the sin" reverses the conditional construction naturally but changes the article ("the sin" vs. "sin"). "Lest they bear sin" is preferred.

**V. 9 — "Who sanctifies them" — divine title.** All three agents: "who sanctifies them" (*meqadsham*). Unanimous consensus: "who sanctifies them."

**V. 10 — "Sojourner of the priest's household" vs. "tenant of a priest."** EMET: "a tenant of a priest [toshav kohen]." LOGOS: "sojourner of the priest's household." PNEUMA: "No guest staying with a priest." Consensus: "sojourner of the priest's household" — LOGOS. The MT's *toshav kohen* is literally "a sojourner/resident of a priest" — a free non-family member residing in the priestly household. LOGOS's "sojourner of the priest's household" preserves both the term and the household-boundary logic. EMET's "tenant" is defensible but introduces a modern property-rental connotation not present in *toshav*. PNEUMA's "guest" understates the residential permanence implied by *toshav*. "Sojourner of the priest's household" is preferred.

**V. 10 — "Hired servant" vs. "hired worker."** EMET: "hired worker [sachir]." LOGOS: "hired servant." PNEUMA: "hired worker." Consensus: "hired servant" — LOGOS. The MT's *sachir* is a hired laborer. LOGOS's "hired servant" is the established formal rendering (ESV). EMET's and PNEUMA's "hired worker" is equally defensible, but "hired servant" is the more formal legal register term consistent with the project's vocabulary for this context.

**V. 11 — "Buys a person with his money" vs. "buys a slave."** EMET: "buys a person [nefesh] with his silver [qinyan kaspo]." LOGOS: "buys a slave as his property with money." PNEUMA: "buys a slave with his own money." Consensus: "buys a person with his money" — adjusted from EMET/LOGOS. The MT has *qinyan kaspo* ("acquisition of his silver") applied to a *nefesh* ("person/soul"). EMET's "buys a person" preserves the MT's *nefesh* without the interpretive gloss "slave." LOGOS's "buys a slave as his property" is accurate but imports "slave" and "as his property" beyond the literal *nefesh*. PNEUMA's "buys a slave with his own money" similarly adds "slave." The consensus preserves *nefesh* as "a person" while making clear the transactional nature with "buys." The context makes clear this is a slave-purchase; the note records this.

**V. 11 — "Those born in his house" vs. "one born of his house."** EMET: "one born of his house [ylid beito]." LOGOS: "those who are born in his house." PNEUMA: "a slave is born in his household." Consensus: "those born in his house" — LOGOS adjusted. The MT's *ylid beito* is "one born of his house," referring to house-born slaves. LOGOS's plural "those" is appropriate since the category is general. PNEUMA explicitly adds "slave" — an interpretive addition not in the MT at this point. "Those born in his house" is the clearest formulation.

**V. 11 — "May eat of his food" vs. "may eat of his bread."** EMET: "they may eat of his bread [lachmo]." LOGOS: "may eat of his food." PNEUMA: "may eat his food." Consensus: "may eat of his food" — LOGOS. The MT's *be-lachmo* ("of his bread/food") — *lechem* is "bread" but functions as "food" in general in this type of expression. LOGOS's "his food" is the natural English rendering. EMET's "his bread" is literal and defensible but slightly archaic as a general food-reference in English. "His food" is preferred.

**V. 12 — "Marries an outsider" vs. "becomes the wife of an outsider."** EMET: "becomes the wife of an outsider [le-ish zar]." LOGOS: "marries an outsider." PNEUMA: "marries an outsider." Consensus: "marries an outsider" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT has *ki tihyeh le-ish zar* ("when she belongs/comes to be a man who is an outsider"), which is the idiomatic Hebrew expression for marriage. "Marries an outsider" is the natural English equivalent and preserves the key term *zar* ("outsider"). EMET's "becomes the wife of" is accurate but more formal than necessary.

**V. 13 — "Has no offspring" vs. "has no children."** EMET: "has no offspring [zera]." LOGOS: "has no children." PNEUMA: "with no children." Consensus: "has no offspring" — EMET. The MT's *zera* is "seed/offspring/progeny," the same term used throughout the Holiness Code for descendants. "Offspring" preserves the legal scope of *zera* (which encompasses all descendants, not merely minor children). LOGOS's "children" and PNEUMA's "no children" are adequate but slightly narrower than the Hebrew's scope in a legal context where the childlessness affects household-status inheritance.

**V. 13 — "As in her youth" — position.** EMET: "as in her youth [ki-vine'ureha]." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "as in her youth." All agree on this phrase. Consensus: "as in her youth" — universal.

**V. 13 — "She may eat of her father's food" vs. "from her father's bread."** EMET: "from her father's bread [mi-lechem avihah] she may eat." LOGOS: "she may eat of her father's food." PNEUMA: "she may eat her father's food." Consensus: "she may eat of her father's food" — LOGOS. As in v.11, *lechem* functions here as general food; "his food" is the natural English rendering. "Of her father's food" (LOGOS) is slightly more formal than PNEUMA's "her father's food" and preserves the partitive *mi-* ("of").

**V. 14 — "Inadvertently" — agreement.** All three agents: EMET "inadvertently," LOGOS "inadvertently," PNEUMA "by mistake." Consensus: "inadvertently" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT's *bi-shgagah* is the standard term for unintentional error throughout the priestly legislation. "Inadvertently" is the established formal rendering (ESV, NASB). PNEUMA's "by mistake" is acceptable colloquially but less precise than *bi-shgagah*'s legal register.

**V. 14 — "Add a fifth of its value" vs. "add its fifth."** EMET: "add its fifth [chamishito] to it." LOGOS: "add a fifth of its value to it." PNEUMA: "replacing it with something equivalent and adding a fifth of its value on top." Consensus: "add a fifth of its value to it" — LOGOS. The MT's *chamishito* is literally "its fifth" — the 20% restitution surcharge. LOGOS's "a fifth of its value" makes the economic calculation explicit and natural. EMET's "its fifth" is precise but requires more interpretive work from the reader. PNEUMA introduces "replacing it with something equivalent" — this paraphrases the implicit restitution of the principal, but the MT's verse simply says "add its fifth" and "give the holy thing to the priest"; PNEUMA's expansion, while theologically reasonable, goes beyond the verse's specific formulation.

**V. 15 — "Which they contribute to the LORD."** EMET: "which they contribute [asher yarimu — from rum, 'to lift/contribute'] to the LORD." LOGOS: "which they contribute to the LORD." PNEUMA: "be treated as common things." Consensus: "which they contribute to the LORD" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA omits the relative clause "which they contribute to the LORD" in v.15 and instead incorporates it interpretively into the next clause. The MT's syntactic division between vv.15 and 16 is preserved in the consensus.

**V. 16 — "Bear iniquity and guilt" vs. "bear the iniquity of guilt."** EMET: "cause them to bear the iniquity of guilt [avon ashmah]." LOGOS: "cause them to bear iniquity and guilt." PNEUMA: "bring guilt upon themselves." Consensus: "cause them to bear iniquity and guilt" — LOGOS. The MT has *avon ashmah* — a construct phrase combining *avon* ("iniquity/guilt of culpable act") with *ashmah* ("guilt/liability"). LOGOS's "iniquity and guilt" renders both terms. EMET's "iniquity of guilt" treats it as a construct chain, which is grammatically correct but slightly awkward. PNEUMA's "bring guilt upon themselves" collapses both terms and shifts the subject. "Bear iniquity and guilt" is the clearest consensus rendering.

**V. 17 — Second divine speech formula.** All three agents: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying." PNEUMA collapses this and vv.18ff. into a single block with v.1. Consensus: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" — EMET/LOGOS. The threefold *vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe* structure (vv.1, 17, 26) is the chapter's literary skeleton and must be preserved as distinct verse markers.

**V. 18 — "Any man of the house of Israel or of the sojourners in Israel."** EMET: "Any man [ish ish] from the house of Yisra'el or from the sojourners [ha-ger]." LOGOS: "When any man of the house of Israel or of the sojourners in Israel." PNEUMA: "When anyone from the house of Israel or any foreigner living in Israel." Consensus: "When any man of the house of Israel or of the sojourners in Israel" — LOGOS. The MT's *ish ish mi-beit yisrael u-min ha-ger be-yisrael* is the standard distributive formula ("any man... or from the sojourner"). LOGOS renders this most naturally. PNEUMA's "any foreigner" for *ha-ger* conflates the resident *ger* (sojourner, a semi-insider) with a generic foreigner — but at v.25 the *ben-nekhar* (true foreigner) is distinguished from the *ger*. The *ger* in v.18 is the integrated resident alien who may bring offerings. PNEUMA's rendering blurs this important distinction.

**V. 18 — "In fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering."** EMET: "for any of their vows or for any of their freewill offerings." LOGOS: "in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering." PNEUMA: "whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering." Consensus: "whether in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering" — LOGOS/PNEUMA adjusted. LOGOS's "in fulfillment of a vow" captures the *le-fallei neder* (fulfilling a declared vow) well. EMET's "for any of their vows" preserves the plural but is slightly more diffuse. The consensus uses "whether in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering" to cover both categories clearly.

**V. 19 — "For your acceptance, it shall be" vs. "to be accepted you shall offer."** EMET: "for your acceptance [li-retsone'khem] a male without blemish." LOGOS: "to be accepted you shall offer a male without blemish." PNEUMA: "you must present a male without defect from the cattle, the sheep, or the goats, if you want it accepted." Consensus: "for your acceptance, it shall be a male without blemish" — EMET structure preferred. The MT's *li-retsone'khem zachar tamim* places the acceptance-purpose (*li-retsonekhem*) before the content specification (*zachar tamim*). The consensus preserves this word-order, which emphasizes that acceptance is the governing criterion. LOGOS's inversion ("to be accepted you shall offer") is grammatically equivalent but restructures the MT's emphasis. PNEUMA's "if you want it accepted" is too conditional — the MT presents it as a statement of requirement, not a condition.

**V. 19 — "Without blemish" vs. "without defect."** EMET and LOGOS: "without blemish [tamim]." PNEUMA: "without defect." Consensus: "without blemish" — EMET/LOGOS. *Tamim* in sacrificial contexts is the established "without blemish" (ESV, NASB, KJV), which also links to the priestly blemish (*mum*) vocabulary of chapter 21. PNEUMA's "without defect" (NIV) is acceptable but breaks the terminological link between *tamim* (animal) and *mum* (defect) that runs through vv.19–25 and the parallel with ch.21.

**V. 20 — "For it will not be accepted for you."** All three agents agree on the substance. EMET: "for it will not be for acceptance for you." LOGOS: "for it will not be accepted for you." PNEUMA: "because it will not be accepted on your behalf." Consensus: "for it will not be accepted for you" — LOGOS. The MT's *ki lo le-ratson yihyeh lakhem* is "for it will not be for favor/acceptance for you." LOGOS renders this cleanly. EMET's "for it will not be for acceptance" is more literal; PNEUMA's "on your behalf" for *lakhem* is a slight paraphrase. "For you" is the literal *lakhem*.

**V. 21 — "Peace offerings" vs. "fellowship offering."** EMET: "peace offering [zevach shelamim]." LOGOS: "peace offerings." PNEUMA: "fellowship offering." Consensus: "peace offerings" — EMET/LOGOS. *Zevach shelamim* is the standard "sacrifice of peace offerings" throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 3; 7). "Peace offerings" is the established formal rendering (ESV, NASB). PNEUMA's "fellowship offering" (NIV) is an interpretive rendering emphasizing the communal meal aspect, but "peace offerings" is the project-established term for *shelamim*.

**V. 21 — "To fulfill a vow" — le-fallei neder.** EMET: "to fulfill a vow [le-fallei neder]." LOGOS: "to fulfill a vow." PNEUMA: "to fulfill a special vow." Consensus: "to fulfill a vow" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "special" to reflect the *pala* root (extraordinary/distinct), which is a legitimate note but an addition beyond the MT's plain *neder*. The notes record the *pala* nuance; the translation itself keeps "a vow."

**V. 22 — Blemish list — "broken" vs. "fractured."** EMET: "blind or broken [shavur]." LOGOS: "broken." PNEUMA: "injured." Consensus: "broken" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT's *shavur* is "broken/fractured." LOGOS correctly renders "broken." PNEUMA's "injured" is too general and loses the specific fracture sense.

**V. 22 — "Maimed" vs. "having a slit/cut" for charutz.** EMET: "maimed or having a running sore [charutz]." LOGOS: "maimed." PNEUMA: "maimed." Consensus: "maimed" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT's *charutz* is a difficult term (EMET's note: "incised/gashed/slit"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "maimed" as a general rendering for this unclear term. This is a case where the standard formal tradition ("maimed" — ESV, NASB) is preferred over EMET's more speculative specific gloss.

**V. 22 — "Running sore" vs. "discharge" vs. "an itch" for yabbelet.** EMET: "having a running sore [yabbelet]." LOGOS: "having a discharge." PNEUMA: "running sores." Consensus: "having a running sore" — EMET/PNEUMA. The MT's *yabbelet* is a wart, growth, or running sore. LOGOS's "discharge" may conflate *yabbelet* with the *zov* (discharge) of the previous section. "Running sore" (EMET/PNEUMA) better captures the skin-surface growth or eruption sense of *yabbelet*.

**V. 22 — "Rash" vs. "itch" vs. "scabby" for garav.** EMET: "scabby [garav]." LOGOS: "an itch." PNEUMA: "a rash." Consensus: "a rash" — PNEUMA. The MT's *garav* is a skin condition (same term in the priestly blemish list at Lev 21:20) involving eruption or scabbing. "Rash" (PNEUMA) is more natural modern English. LOGOS's "itch" is too symptom-specific. EMET's "scabby" is descriptive but clinical.

**V. 22 — "Scabs" vs. "skin discharge" for yalefet.** EMET: "with a skin discharge [yalefet]." LOGOS: "scabs." PNEUMA: "festering skin." Consensus: "scabs" — LOGOS. The MT's *yalefet* (also in Lev 21:20) is a dry, crusty skin condition. "Scabs" (LOGOS) is the most natural English rendering. EMET's "skin discharge" overreads *yalefet* toward a liquid sense; PNEUMA's "festering skin" implies active infection. "Scabs" is preferred.

**V. 22 — "Food offering" vs. "fire-offering" for isheh.** EMET: "a fire-offering [isheh]." LOGOS: "a food offering." PNEUMA: "a food offering." Consensus: "a food offering" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT's *isheh* (traditionally "fire-offering," from *esh*, "fire") is rendered in modern formal translations as "food offering" (ESV, NASB) to capture its broader sense as an offering presented to the LORD. EMET's "fire-offering" is etymologically defensible but the "food offering" rendering is more consistent with modern consensus scholarship and the project's formal equivalent choices elsewhere in Leviticus.

**V. 23 — "A bull or a lamb" vs. "an ox or a sheep."** EMET: "an ox [shor] or a sheep [seh]." LOGOS: "A bull or a lamb." PNEUMA: "a bull or lamb." Consensus: "A bull or a lamb" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT's *shor* is "bull/ox" and *seh* can mean "sheep" or "lamb." LOGOS's "bull" is preferred for *shor* in sacrifice contexts. *Seh* here is rendered "lamb" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) in the context of the vow/freewill offering distinction, though "sheep" would also be accurate. "Bull or a lamb" is the standard formal rendering (ESV) for this pairing.

**V. 23 — "A limb too long or too short" — agreement.** EMET: "one limb extended [sarua] or contracted [qalutt]." LOGOS: "a limb too long or too short." PNEUMA: "a limb too long or too short." Consensus: "a limb too long or too short" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT's *sarua ve-qalutt* describes proportional limb asymmetry (elongated/shortened). LOGOS's "too long or too short" is the most accessible English rendering and is used in ESV. EMET's "extended or contracted" is more literal but less natural.

**V. 24 — "Testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut."** EMET: "has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut [machutz u-kathit u-nathiq ve-kharet]." LOGOS: "has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut." PNEUMA: "with damaged, crushed, torn, or cut genitals." Consensus: "has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT's four terms (*machutz*, *kathit*, *nathiq*, *kharet*) describe a spectrum of testicular injury from bruising to castration. Both EMET and LOGOS render the four terms precisely. PNEUMA's "damaged" for *machutz* ("bruised/compressed") is vaguer than the MT; PNEUMA's "genitals" for what the MT specifies as testicles (*eshkhim*, implied from the four injury terms) is a generalization. The specific anatomical rendering is preferred in the consensus.

**V. 25 — "Obtained from a foreigner" vs. "from the hand of a foreigner's son."** EMET: "from the hand of a foreigner's son [ben-nekhar]." LOGOS: "animals obtained from a foreigner." PNEUMA: "such animals from a foreigner." Consensus: "any such animals obtained from a foreigner" — LOGOS/PNEUMA adjusted. The MT's *mi-yad ben-nekhar* ("from the hand of a son of a foreigner") specifies a non-Israelite non-resident alien (distinct from the *ger* of v.18). LOGOS's "obtained from a foreigner" captures the transactional sense (*mi-yad* = "from the hand of") without the slightly awkward English of EMET's "foreigner's son." The consensus adds "any such" to reflect the *mi-kol-elleh* ("from any of these") in the MT.

**V. 25 — "Their corruption is in them" vs. "their deformity."** EMET: "their corruption is in them [mashchatam bahem]." LOGOS: "because of their deformity." PNEUMA: "since they are corrupt — they have a defect." Consensus: "for their corruption is in them — there is a blemish in them" — EMET structure with both clauses. The MT has *ki mashchatam bahem* ("for their corruption/ruin is in them") and *mum bam* ("a blemish in them") — two parallel clauses. EMET preserves both. LOGOS collapses "corruption" into "deformity" and merges the two clauses. PNEUMA preserves both but reorders them. The consensus preserves both clauses in the MT order: "for their corruption is in them — there is a blemish in them."

**V. 27 — "A bull or a sheep or a goat" vs. "an ox or a sheep or a goat."** EMET: "an ox or a sheep or a goat [shor o kesev o ez]." LOGOS: "a bull or a sheep or a goat." PNEUMA: "a calf, a lamb, or a goat." Consensus: "a bull or a sheep or a goat" — LOGOS. The MT's *shor* is "bull/ox," *kesev* is "sheep/lamb," *ez* is "goat." LOGOS uses "bull" consistently for *shor* in this context. PNEUMA's "calf, lamb" introduces age-specific terms (*kesev* and *shor* do not specify young animals in this legal context; the minimum age is the subject of the verse itself, making PNEUMA's "calf/lamb" interpretively circular). "Bull or a sheep or a goat" follows LOGOS.

**V. 27 — "Remain seven days with its mother" vs. "under its mother."** EMET: "seven days under its mother [tachat immo]." LOGOS: "remain seven days with its mother." PNEUMA: "remain with its mother for seven days." Consensus: "remain seven days with its mother" — LOGOS. The MT's *tachat immo* is "under its mother" (EMET preserves the literal *tachat*, "under"), which evokes the nursing/sheltering posture. LOGOS renders "with its mother," which is the natural English equivalent (LOGOS notes the LXX uses *hypo* = "under," which is slightly more literal). "With its mother" is standard English rendering (ESV, NASB) for this verse and is adopted in the consensus. The note records the LXX's *hypo* ("under").

**V. 27 — "Acceptable as a food offering" vs. "acceptable as a fire-offering."** EMET: "accepted as a fire-offering [le-isheh]." LOGOS: "acceptable as a food offering." PNEUMA: "acceptable as a food offering." Consensus: "acceptable as a food offering" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. Following the v.22 ruling on *isheh*, "food offering" is the consensus rendering.

**V. 28 — "Ox or a sheep" vs. "cow or a sheep" / "an ox or a sheep."** EMET: "an ox [shor] or a sheep [seh]." LOGOS: "an ox or a sheep." PNEUMA: "a cow or a sheep." Consensus: "an ox or a sheep" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT's *shor o seh* — *shor* is used generically for cattle (either sex); "ox" is the conventional formal rendering (ESV, NASB). PNEUMA's "cow" introduces a female-specific term not required by *shor*, which is grammatically masculine but used generically. "An ox or a sheep" is the standard rendering.

**V. 28 — "And its young" vs. "and its offspring."** EMET: "it and its offspring [oto ve-et-beno]." LOGOS: "and her young." PNEUMA: "along with its young." Consensus: "and its young" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT's *oto ve-et-beno* is "it and its son/young." LOGOS's "her young" introduces a female pronoun for the parent animal that is not in the MT (the parent animal's sex is unspecified — it may be either cow or bull). "Its young" is preferred as gender-neutral and consistent with the MT. The full clause: "an ox or a sheep and its young both on the same day."

**V. 28 — "Both on the same day" vs. "in one day."** EMET: "on the same day [be-yom echad]." LOGOS: "both in one day." PNEUMA: "on the same day." Consensus: "both on the same day" — LOGOS/PNEUMA adjusted. The MT's *be-yom echad* is "in one day." "Both" (LOGOS) is an appropriate clarifier that both the parent and offspring are in view. "On the same day" is more natural English than "in one day" (LOGOS). The consensus uses "both on the same day."

**V. 29 — "Thank offering" vs. "thanksgiving offering."** EMET: "thank offering [zevach todah]." LOGOS: "thanksgiving." PNEUMA: "thanksgiving offering." Consensus: "thank offering" — EMET. The MT's *zevach todah* is "sacrifice of acknowledgment/thanksgiving." "Thank offering" is the established project convention for *todah* and is consistent with the formal translation tradition (ESV uses "thanksgiving offering"; NASB "sacrifice of thanksgiving"). "Thank offering" is more compact and is the consensus.

**V. 29 — "So that it may be accepted on your behalf" vs. "for your acceptance."** EMET: "for your acceptance you shall sacrifice it [li-rtsone'khem]." LOGOS: "so that you may be accepted." PNEUMA: "in a way that it will be accepted on your behalf." Consensus: "so that it may be accepted on your behalf" — adjusted. The MT's *li-rtsone'khem* is "for your acceptance/favor" — the *ratson* formula that governs sacrificial acceptability throughout chs. 19–22. LOGOS's "so that you may be accepted" implies personal acceptance of the worshiper; EMET's "for your acceptance" preserves the formal *ratson* term. PNEUMA's "in a way that it will be accepted on your behalf" is the most explicit. The consensus adopts "so that it may be accepted on your behalf" as capturing both the sacrificial act (the offering) and its beneficiary (the worshiper) without collapsing *ratson* into personal approval.

**V. 30 — "On the same day" / "leave none until morning."** All three agents agree on the substance. EMET: "On that very day it shall be eaten; you shall not leave any of it until morning." LOGOS: "It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until morning." PNEUMA: "It must be eaten that same day — leave none of it until morning." Consensus: "It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until morning" — LOGOS. EMET's "On that very day" (*be-yom ha-hu*) is slightly more emphatic ("that very day") than LOGOS's "on the same day," but "the same day" is the more idiomatic English for the same-day consumption rule. PNEUMA's dash-construction is acceptable but less formal.

**V. 31 — "You shall keep my commandments and do them."** EMET: "And you shall keep my commandments and do them." LOGOS: "So you shall keep my commandments and do them." PNEUMA: "Keep my commands and carry them out." Consensus: "You shall keep my commandments and do them" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT has *u-shmartem et-mitsvotai va-asitem otam* — "and you shall keep my commandments and do them." "Commandments" (*mitsvot*) is the correct term here (as distinct from "statutes" *chuqqim* or "rules" *mishpatim*). "Do them" preserves the MT's *asah* ("to do/make"); PNEUMA's "carry them out" is an expansion. The *vav*-conjunction is preserved as "And you shall keep."

**V. 32 — "That I may be sanctified" vs. "and I will be sanctified."** EMET: "and I will be sanctified [ve-niqdashhti] in the midst of the sons of Yisra'el." LOGOS: "that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel." PNEUMA: "for I must be treated as holy among the Israelites." Consensus: "that I may be sanctified in the midst of the sons of Israel" — EMET/LOGOS adjusted. The MT's *ve-niqdashhti* is the Nifal of *q-d-sh* — "and I shall be/will be sanctified/hallowed." LOGOS's "that I may be sanctified" renders this as a purpose clause (the purpose of not profaning the Name). EMET's "and I will be sanctified" renders it as a result clause. Both readings are defensible; the purpose clause reading (LOGOS) is slightly preferred as it captures the human-divine interactive dimension of kiddush ha-Shem: Israel's not-profaning is the means by which YHWH is sanctified. PNEUMA's "I must be treated as holy" is an interpretive paraphrase that captures the nuance but loses the Nifal passive construction. "Be tokh" ("in the midst of") is preserved over LOGOS's "among" to reflect the communal-spatial dimension of the sanctification.

**V. 32 — "I am the LORD who sanctifies you" [meqadishkhem].** All three agents: "I am the LORD who sanctifies you." Unanimous consensus.

**V. 33 — "Who brought you out of the land of Egypt."** All three agents agree. EMET: "who brought you out from the land of Egypt." LOGOS: "who brought you out of the land of Egypt." PNEUMA: "who brought you out of Egypt." Consensus: "who brought you out of the land of Egypt" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT's *me-erets Mitsrayim* ("from the land of Egypt") is preserved in full. PNEUMA's "out of Egypt" drops *erets* ("land"); the full formula "the land of Egypt" is the project standard for this Exodus-identification formula (cf. Lev 11:45; 19:36; 25:38; 26:13).

**V. 33 — "To be your God."** All three agents: "to be your God." EMET: "to be your God [li-hyot lakhem l'Elohim]." LOGOS: "to be your God." PNEUMA: "to be your God." Unanimous consensus.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On ve-yinnazeru (v.2) — the nazir-root in priestly legislation:** The verb *yinnazeru* is the Nifal of *n-z-r* — the root that gives us *nazir* ("Nazirite," the separated one), the *nezer* (crown) of the high priest, and here the priest's required active withdrawal from holy things when ritually unqualified. The paradox is deliberate: the same root governing consecrated attachment elsewhere here governs withdrawal from the holy. Only the priest who separates himself from holy things when unclean may properly approach them when clean. The LXX renders this with *phylassontai* ("they shall guard/keep") in some manuscripts, losing the specific *nazir*-resonance; Vaticanus uses *apochoresthosan* ("they shall be separated"). The consensus preserves "separate themselves" as the translation that best honors the root's theological weight.

**On tamim (vv.19–25) — the shared standard of priest and animal:** The term *tamim* ("without blemish/complete") runs through the animal-sacrifice legislation of vv.17–25 as the animal's required quality, directly paralleling the priest's required bodily integrity (*tamim* / *mum* = "no blemish") from chapter 21. The structural homology is one of the Holiness Code's most deliberate literary features: both the officiant (priest) and the offering (animal) must be *tamim* before the altar. The LXX renders *tamim* as *amōmon* ("without blemish") throughout this section — the same word used in 1 Peter 1:19 for Christ as "a lamb without blemish and without spot," a NT Christological appropriation of the Levitical sacrificial standard.

**On chillul ha-Shem / kiddush ha-Shem (vv.2, 9, 15, 32):** The verb *chalal* (Piel, "to profane/desecrate") appears four times in the chapter (vv.2, 9, 15, 32), each time in relation to the priestly handling of holy things or the divine name itself. The climactic occurrence at v.32 — *ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi* ("you shall not profane my holy name") — introduces the concept of *chillul ha-Shem* (profanation of the Name) with its positive counterpart *kiddush ha-Shem* (sanctification of the Name) implicit in *ve-niqdashhti* ("I will be sanctified"). This pairing becomes foundational in later Jewish theology: every action either profanes or sanctifies YHWH's name as perceived by Israel and the nations. The priestly-ritual regulations of this chapter are not mere procedural requirements — they are the concrete enactment of the Name's honor or dishonor in the community.

**On the sojourner (ger) and the foreigner (ben-nekhar) — v.18 vs. v.25:** The chapter makes a significant distinction between the *ger* (vv.18ff.) and the *ben-nekhar* (v.25). The *ger* is the resident alien who has integrated into the covenant community and may bring offerings — subject to the same *tamim* standard as any Israelite. The *ben-nekhar* is the true foreigner from whom one may not accept animals for sacrifice at all, because "their corruption is in them" (*mashchatam bahem*). The distinction reflects the gradated access system of the Holiness Code: the *ger* who has come under Israel's covenant obligations receives covenant privileges; the *ben-nekhar* who remains outside does not. The LXX renders *ger* as *prosēlytos* (proselyte) throughout this section, reflecting a later religious-legal category.

**On oto ve-et-beno (v.28) — the compassion-law:** The prohibition of slaughtering a parent animal and its offspring on the same day is one of the Torah's explicit animal-welfare laws (alongside Deut 22:6–7, the mother-bird law). The MT uses *oto ve-et-beno* ("it and its son/young") without specifying which is to be slaughtered first — only that both may not be slaughtered on the same day. Rabbinic tradition extended the prohibition to cover any mother-offspring pair regardless of which is slaughtered first. Maimonides (Guide 3:48) attributed the law to compassion for the feelings of the mother animal; Nahmanides saw it as preventing the hardening of human capacity for cruelty. Whatever the rationale, the law creates a pause within the sacrificial system: even in a system that requires animal death, something like compassion imposes limits.

**On the three-part closing (vv.31–33):** The chapter's theological climax in vv.31–33 recapitulates the Holiness Code's structural logic in three movements: (1) commandment ("keep my commandments and do them"); (2) Name-sanctification ("you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified in the midst of the sons of Israel"); (3) Exodus-grounding ("I am the LORD who sanctifies you, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God; I am the LORD"). The progression moves from human obligation → divine holiness-display → covenant-historical foundation. Every regulation in the chapter flows backward from this conclusion: the complex purity rules and animal-blemish catalogues are not autonomous requirements but the practical shape of a relationship established by the Exodus and maintained through the ongoing sanctification of the Name.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 22:1–33. All 33 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_022.md`.*
